records_request_details.description_of_records_being_requested:
I am requesting access to and copies of the following public records maintained by the Clay County Public Health Center (CGCPHC) under the Missouri Sunshine Law, Chapter 610, RSMo:

1. Aggregate communicable disease case data for Clay County, Missouri, broken down by month, from January 1, 2022 through June 30, 2024, for the following reportable conditions: COVID-19, influenza (all types), RSV, and sexually transmitted infections (chlamydia, gonorrhea, and syphilis). I am specifically requesting: 
   - Monthly case counts by disease
   - Age group breakdowns if available (e.g., 0–4, 5–17, 18–49, 50–64, 65+)
   - Any available breakdowns by ZIP code or census tract (with no personally identifiable information)

2. Any written or electronic reports, dashboards, or summary documents prepared for the Clay County Public Health Board or county commissioners that present or discuss the above disease trends during this same period (Jan 1, 2022 – Jun 30, 2024). This includes:
   - Monthly or quarterly surveillance reports
   - PowerPoint presentations or PDF briefings
   - Internal memos or situation reports summarizing trends for leadership, provided that any individual-level identifying information is redacted.

3. Policies, protocols, or written procedures in effect during this same time frame (Jan 1, 2022 – Jun 30, 2024) related to:
   - How communicable disease data (including COVID-19, flu, RSV, and STIs) are collected, verified, and reported to the state
   - Criteria for when CGCPHC issues public health advisories, press releases, or community alerts related to spikes or outbreaks in those conditions
   - Any written standard operating procedures for protecting patient privacy in public data reporting (including de-identification and cell suppression rules).

4. Copies of any public-facing dashboards, data visualizations, or fact sheets that CGCPHC has produced or disseminated to the public (online or in print) that show county-level data for COVID-19, influenza, RSV, or STIs during the requested period.

I am not requesting any records that contain personally identifiable information such as names, full dates of birth, street-level addresses, Social Security numbers, medical record numbers, or any other direct identifiers. I am only requesting aggregate or properly de-identified records and policy documents. If any of the requested records contain confidential or exempt information, I respectfully ask that those portions be redacted and the remainder of the records be provided.

If some of these records are already publicly available online, I would appreciate being directed to the exact URLs or online repositories where they can be accessed, and I would still like copies of any internal summary reports or policy documents that are not otherwise posted online.
records_request_details.time_frame_requesting: From 2022-01-01 through 2024-06-30 (inclusive). If records are organized differently (e.g., by calendar year or fiscal year), please provide the closest equivalent coverage for 2022, 2023, and January through June 2024.
records_request_details.how_and_why_the_information_will_be_used:
I am requesting that any fees associated with this request be waived because the records will be used for non-commercial, educational, and public-information purposes that will directly benefit Clay County residents and the broader Missouri public.

By way of background, I am a freelance public health journalist and community health advocate based in Kansas City, Missouri. Over the past several years, I have written explanatory articles and data-driven blog posts about local health issues, including vaccine access, school health policies, and seasonal disease trends. I publish most of my work on a small, ad-free community website that I maintain (NorthlandHealthWatch.org) as well as through occasional guest columns in local outlets such as The Kansas City Beacon and neighborhood association newsletters. I do not sell the data or use it for marketing; my work is supported primarily through small reader donations and occasional grants from nonprofit organizations focused on civic information.

The information I am requesting will be used to create a series of accessible, data-focused articles and visualizations that help residents understand:

1. How communicable diseases such as COVID-19, flu, RSV, and STIs have been trending in Clay County over the last two and a half years.
2. Whether there are particular times of year when certain infections spike, and what practical steps residents and community organizations can take in anticipation of those patterns (for example, timing flu shot clinics or school information campaigns).
3. How Clay County’s patterns compare, in general terms, to state and national trends, so that residents have context for what they are hearing in the broader news.
4. What policies and procedures the Health Center uses to decide when and how to communicate about outbreaks or surges, which can build public trust by making these processes more transparent.

I intend to use the data to build simple charts and maps that can be easily understood by non-experts. For example, I plan to:
- Create line graphs that show monthly case counts for COVID-19, flu, and RSV, so parents and school staff can see what to expect during typical winter months.
- Develop neighborhood- or ZIP-code-level visual summaries (with appropriate aggregation to avoid any privacy issues) that can help community organizations, such as churches and local nonprofits, target outreach efforts like free testing events or health education sessions.
- Write plain-language explainers that walk through the Health Center’s policies on data collection and privacy protections, so that residents understand what information is (and is not) shared.

In addition, I expect to share the resulting articles, graphics, and explanations with:
- Local parent-teacher organizations (PTOs) and school health staff who have previously asked me for clearer, localized information to share with families.
- Neighborhood associations in Liberty, Gladstone, and surrounding communities, many of which distribute monthly email newsletters or printed flyers.
- Regional public health students and faculty at local institutions (such as UMKC and local community colleges), who have expressed interest in using locally relevant examples in classroom discussions.

Because this project is entirely non-commercial and focused on improving public understanding of local health trends and public health decision-making, paying significant fees out of pocket would be a hardship and would limit my ability to complete and widely share this work. I am not requesting the records for litigation, business development, advertising, or any other commercial activity. I will credit the Clay County Public Health Center as the data source in any published materials and will make the resulting analyses available free of charge to the public.

For these reasons, I respectfully request that you waive any fees associated with searching for, reviewing, and copying the requested records, or, if a full waiver is not possible, that you substantially reduce the fees to the greatest extent allowed under the Missouri Sunshine Law for requests that serve the public interest.
